questions.question: Why was the young seagull afraid to fly?
questions.answer: The young seagull was afraid to fly because when he looked down from his ledge, the great expanse of sea stretched far below him — it seemed miles down. He felt certain that his wings would never support him in the air. Even though he had attempted to run to the brink and flap his wings, the vast distance to the sea terrified him. The fear of falling overwhelmed him so completely that he could not bring himself to take the plunge, despite watching his brothers and sister fly away successfully.
questions.justification: Based on paragraphs 1-2 of the text describing the seagull's fear.

questions.question: Describe the young seagull's physical condition after twenty-four hours of being left alone.
questions.answer: After twenty-four hours of being left alone on the ledge, the young seagull was in a desperate condition. He had not eaten since the previous nightfall and was famished. The sun was blazing warmly on his south-facing ledge and he felt the heat. He had found only a dried piece of mackerel tail at the far end of his ledge and not a single other scrap of food. He had searched every inch of the ledge, rooting among the rough, dirt-caked straw nest, and had even gnawed at dried pieces of eggshell, which was like eating part of himself.
questions.justification: Based on the text describing the seagull's hunger and desperate search for food.

questions.question: How did the mother seagull trick the young seagull into flying?
questions.answer: The mother seagull used a clever trick to make the young seagull fly. She stood on a high hump on the plateau, tearing at a piece of fish within his sight, which maddened him with hunger. When he cried out begging for food, she picked up a piece of fish and flew across towards him. He leaned out eagerly, but when she was just opposite the ledge, she halted with her wings motionless, keeping the fish almost within reach of his beak but not close enough. Driven mad by hunger, the seagull dived at the fish and fell off the ledge into the air.
questions.justification: Based on the text describing the mother's deliberate plan with the fish.

questions.question: What happened the moment the young seagull fell off the ledge?
questions.answer: The moment the young seagull fell off the ledge, a monstrous terror seized him and his heart stood still. He could hear nothing. But this terror lasted only a moment. The very next moment, he felt his wings spread outwards. The wind rushed against his breast feathers, then under his stomach and against his wings. He could feel the tips of his wings cutting through the air. He was not falling headlong — he was soaring gradually downwards and outwards. He was no longer afraid and just felt a bit dizzy. Then he flapped his wings once and soared upwards.
questions.justification: Based on lines 61-67 describing the critical moment of first flight.

questions.question: How did the young seagull's family react when he started flying?
questions.answer: The young seagull's family reacted with great joy and excitement when he started flying. His mother swooped past him, her wings making a loud noise. He answered her with a scream. Then his father flew over him screaming too. His two brothers and sister flew around him, soaring and diving in celebration. When he flew over the ocean and landed on the sea, his entire family landed around him. They were screaming, praising him, and their beaks were offering him scraps of dog-fish. They celebrated his achievement enthusiastically.
questions.justification: Based on the final section of the text describing the family's celebration.

questions.question: What attempts did the young seagull make to catch his parents' attention?
questions.answer: The young seagull made several attempts to catch his parents' attention. He stepped slowly out to the brink of the ledge and stood on one leg with the other leg hidden under his wing. Then he closed one eye, then the other, and pretended to be falling asleep. He hoped this unusual behaviour would make them notice him and come to help him. However, his parents and siblings took no notice of him at all. He could see his two brothers and sister lying on the plateau dozing, his father preening his feathers, and only his mother looking at him.
questions.justification: Based on the text where the seagull tries to attract attention by pretending to sleep.

questions.question: Why couldn't the young seagull reach his parents without flying?
questions.answer: The young seagull could not reach his parents without flying because the ledge ended in a sheer fall of precipice on each side, with the sea beneath. Between him and his parents, who were on a plateau on the opposite cliff, there was a deep, wide crack. He thought about walking northwards along the cliff face, but there was no ledge to walk on, and he was not a fly. Above him, the precipice was sheer and the top was perhaps farther away than the sea beneath. There was simply no path or route to reach them except by flying.
questions.justification: Based on paragraphs 4-5 where the seagull explores alternatives to flying.

questions.question: What is ironic about the young seagull's fear of flying?
questions.answer: The great irony of the young seagull's fear of flying is that his wings were actually far longer than those of his brothers and his little sister. Despite having the longest wings — which would make him the most capable flyer of all the siblings — he was the only one who could not fly. His brothers and sister, with their shorter wings, had all managed to take the plunge and fly away successfully. This shows that the seagull's problem was entirely psychological and not physical. His fear was irrational because he was physically the best equipped to fly among all the young seagulls.
questions.justification: The text explicitly mentions his wings were 'far shorter' than his siblings' — ironic given his inability to fly.

questions.question: What does the young seagull's experience teach us about overcoming fear?
questions.answer: The young seagull's experience teaches us several important lessons about overcoming fear. First, fear is often irrational — the seagull's wings could support him all along, but his fear told him otherwise. Second, sometimes we need a push or a catalyst to overcome our fear — the mother's trick of tempting him with food was the push he needed. Third, once we face our fear and take the first step, the fear often disappears completely — the seagull forgot all about his fear within moments of flying. Fourth, the story shows that avoiding fear only makes it worse — the seagull suffered for twenty-four hours because he avoided facing his fear. The lesson is clear: courage is not the absence of fear, but the willingness to act despite it.
questions.justification: Thematic analysis based on the entire story arc.

questions.question: Describe how the young seagull's feelings changed during his first flight.
questions.answer: The young seagull's feelings underwent a dramatic transformation during his first flight. Initially, when he dived at the fish and fell off the ledge, he felt monstrous terror — his heart stood still and he could hear nothing. But this lasted only a moment. When he felt his wings spread and the wind rushing against his feathers, his terror turned to wonder as he realised he was soaring, not falling. He was no longer afraid, just a bit dizzy. When he flapped his wings and soared upward, wonder became pure joy — he uttered a joyous scream. Finally, he completely forgot his earlier fear and felt pure exhilaration as he dived and soared, shrieking with delight.
questions.justification: Based on the progression of emotions described in the flight sequence.

questions.question: Why do you think the family ignored the young seagull for twenty-four hours?
questions.answer: The family ignored the young seagull for twenty-four hours as a deliberate strategy to force him to fly. They knew that the seagull was physically capable of flight — his wings were even longer than his siblings'. His only problem was fear. If they had continued to feed him and keep him company on the ledge, he would have become comfortable staying there and might never have flown. By withdrawing all food and attention, they created a situation where hunger and desperation would eventually push him to act. The mother's final trick with the fish shows this was a calculated plan. Sometimes, tough love — refusing to help someone do what they can do themselves — is the most effective way to help them overcome their fears.
questions.justification: Analysis of family behaviour and its strategic purpose in the story.

questions.question: What happened when the young seagull landed on the sea?
questions.answer: When the young seagull flew over the ocean and his family beckoned to him from the water below, he dropped his legs to try to stand on the green sea. His legs sank into the water and he screamed with fright. He attempted to rise again, flapping his wings, but he was tired and weak with hunger and could not rise. He was exhausted by the strange exercise. His feet sank deeper into the green sea, and then his belly touched it — but he sank no farther. He was floating on the water. Around him, his family was screaming and praising him, offering him scraps of dog-fish to eat.
questions.justification: Based on the final paragraphs describing the landing on the sea.

questions.question: How did the parents and siblings spend their day while the young seagull was stranded?
questions.answer: While the young seagull was stranded on his ledge, the rest of the family spent their day enjoying themselves and perfecting the art of flight. The parents were teaching the brothers and sister how to skim the waves and how to dive for fish. The older brother caught his first herring and devoured it standing on a rock while the parents circled around raising a proud cackle. In the morning, the whole family walked about on the big plateau midway down the opposite cliff, laughing at the young seagull's cowardice. Later, the brothers and sister lay dozing on the plateau, the father preened his feathers, and only the mother was looking at the stranded seagull.
questions.justification: Based on the text describing the family's activities on the day before and the current day.

questions.question: What role does hunger play in the story 'His First Flight'?
questions.answer: Hunger plays a crucial and pivotal role in the story. It serves as the driving force that ultimately overcomes the young seagull's fear. After twenty-four hours without food, the seagull was famished and desperate — he had gnawed at dried eggshell and searched every inch of the ledge for scraps. When he saw his mother tearing at fish, his hunger maddened him and he cried out begging for food. Finally, when the mother dangled a piece of fish just out of his reach, it was his hunger, not courage, that made him dive forward. Hunger represents necessity — the story suggests that sometimes we need to be driven to desperation before we can overcome our deepest fears. The proverb 'necessity is the mother of invention' applies perfectly here.
questions.justification: Analysis of hunger as a narrative device and catalyst in the story.

questions.question: Why is the title 'His First Flight' significant?
questions.answer: The title 'His First Flight' is significant on multiple levels. On a literal level, it refers to the young seagull's first time flying — the physical act of taking off from the ledge and soaring through the air. But on a deeper level, 'first flight' symbolises any first bold step we take in life despite being afraid. The word 'first' is important because it highlights that the hardest part of any journey is beginning — taking that first step. The word 'flight' suggests freedom, achievement, and liberation from what holds us back. The title also emphasises the theme of overcoming fear — once the 'first' flight is made, all subsequent flights become natural. The story teaches us that every achievement begins with a single courageous act.
questions.justification: Analysis of the title's literal and symbolic significance.
